description: Fix bugs in CLI, plugin-tools, and browser-tools analysis: unbounded module cache in watch mode, parseHex missing RGBA/RRGGBBAA support, SVG style blocks bypassing achromatic validation, formatUptime fractional seconds, Windows path detection, and looksLikeJwt false positives.

userStories.title: Fix parseHex to handle #RRGGBBAA and #RGBA hex colors
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want my SVG icons using 8-digit hex colors (#RRGGBBAA) to be properly validated for achromatic compliance, so that colored icons don't silently bypass the inactive icon check.
acceptanceCriteria: parseHex correctly parses 5-character hex (#RGBA) and 9-character hex (#RRGGBBAA) colors
#FF0000FF (red with full opacity) is recognized as a non-achromatic color by isAchromaticColor
#80808080 (gray with alpha) is recognized as achromatic
generateInactiveIcon converts 8-digit hex colors to grayscale equivalents
userStories.notes:
File: platform/plugin-tools/src/validate-icon.ts, lines 129-149.

Current parseHex only handles length 4 (#RGB) and 7 (#RRGGBB). CSS also supports #RGBA (5 chars) and #RRGGBBAA (9 chars).

Fix: Add two more cases to parseHex:
- Length 5 (#RGBA): parse like #RGB (expand each digit) and ignore the alpha channel for color comparison
- Length 9 (#RRGGBBAA): parse first 6 hex digits as #RRGGBB, ignore the last 2 (alpha) for color comparison

The alpha channel is irrelevant for achromatic detection — we only care about the RGB components.

Test file: platform/plugin-tools/src/validate-icon.test.ts

userStories.title: Detect color properties in SVG <style> blocks for inactive icon validation
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want SVG icons using <style> blocks with colored CSS rules to be flagged during inactive icon validation, so that colored icons don't silently bypass the achromatic check.
acceptanceCriteria: validateInactiveIconColors detects non-achromatic colors in <style> blocks (e.g., `<style>circle { fill: red; }</style>`)
generateInactiveIcon converts colors in <style> blocks to grayscale
Both fill and stroke color properties in <style> blocks are handled
CSS hex colors (#rgb, #rrggbb, #rrggbbaa), named colors, and rgb() in <style> blocks are detected
SVGs with only achromatic colors in <style> blocks pass validation
userStories.notes:
File: platform/plugin-tools/src/validate-icon.ts, lines 309-442.

Both validateInactiveIconColors (around line 309) and generateInactiveIcon (around line 389) only check color-carrying attributes (fill='...', stroke='...') and inline style='...' attributes. They do NOT parse <style> blocks.

Fix approach: Add a function to extract color declarations from <style> blocks in the SVG. Use a regex to find `<style>` content, then extract fill/stroke property values (e.g., `fill:\s*([^;]+)`, `stroke:\s*([^;]+)`). Parse these values through the existing parseColor pipeline.

For validateInactiveIconColors: check all colors in <style> blocks via isAchromaticColor.
For generateInactiveIcon: replace color values in <style> blocks with their grayscale equivalents via colorToGrayscale.

This is an opus-level story because it requires understanding the existing color parsing pipeline and the regex-based SVG attribute processing, then designing a parallel approach for CSS-in-<style> that integrates cleanly.

Test file: platform/plugin-tools/src/validate-icon.test.ts

userStories.title: Fix looksLikeJwt false positives on short dotted strings
userStories.description: As a plugin developer using the site analyzer, I want JWT detection to not flag version strings like '1.2.3' as JWTs, so that the auth analysis produces accurate results.
acceptanceCriteria: looksLikeJwt returns false for short dotted strings like '1.2.3', '1.0.0', 'a.b.c'
looksLikeJwt returns true for real JWTs (header segment >= 20 chars typically)
A minimum segment length of 10 characters is enforced (or first segment is verified to decode to JSON with an 'alg' field)
Unit tests cover version strings, short dotted strings, and real JWT examples

userStories.title: Fix formatUptime fractional seconds display
userStories.description: As a user running opentabs status, I want uptime to display clean values like '2m 3s' instead of '2m 3.456s'.
userStories.notes:
File: platform/cli/src/commands/status.ts, lines 32-43.

The function receives `seconds: number` which can be a float. The modulo operations produce fractional results.

Fix: Add `seconds = Math.floor(seconds);` as the first line of the function body. This is the standard approach for uptime display.

Test file: platform/cli/src/commands/status.test.ts — add a test case with fractional input.

userStories.title: Use path.isAbsolute() instead of startsWith('/') for Windows compatibility
userStories.description: As a plugin developer on Windows, I want opentabs-plugin build to correctly detect absolute paths in config.json's localPlugins, so that plugin path comparison works on Windows.
acceptanceCriteria: resolvePluginPathForComparison uses path.isAbsolute() instead of startsWith('/')
A Windows-style absolute path like 'C:\\plugins\\my-plugin' is correctly recognized as absolute
A Unix-style absolute path '/Users/me/plugins/my-plugin' is still correctly recognized as absolute
Relative paths are still resolved against configDir
userStories.notes:
File: platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts, lines 156-160.

Current code:
```typescript
const resolvePluginPathForComparison = (storedPath: string, configDir: string): string => {
  if (storedPath.startsWith('/')) return storedPath;
  ...
```

Fix: Import `isAbsolute` from 'node:path' and replace `storedPath.startsWith('/')` with `isAbsolute(storedPath)`. The `isAbsolute` function handles both Unix (starts with /) and Windows (starts with drive letter like C:\) absolute paths.

Check that 'node:path' is already imported in the file (it likely is for other functions like `resolve`, `join`).

userStories.title: Mitigate unbounded module cache growth in watch mode
userStories.description: As a plugin developer using --watch mode for extended sessions, I want the build tool to not leak memory through the Node.js module cache, so that long development sessions remain stable.
acceptanceCriteria: The watch mode rebuild does not accumulate unbounded entries in the Node.js module cache
Each rebuild either clears the previous cache entry or uses a bounded cache-busting strategy
Watch mode still correctly picks up source changes on each rebuild
userStories.notes:
File: platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts, around line 1029.

Current code: `await import(\`${entryPoint}?t=${String(Date.now())}\`)` creates a unique module URL on every rebuild. Node.js caches each URL separately, so the cache grows without bound.

Fix approach: Delete the previous cache entry before importing the new one. Node.js ESM modules can be uncached by deleting from the internal module cache, but this is not straightforward for ESM. A simpler approach:

1. Use a fixed small set of cache-busting keys that rotate (e.g., `?t=0` and `?t=1` alternating), so only 2 entries ever exist
2. Or read the file contents directly with `readFile` and use `new Function()` / `eval()` instead of `import()` — but this changes the semantics
3. Or use a worker thread for each rebuild that exits after, freeing the module cache

The rotating key approach is simplest: track a counter that alternates between 0 and 1 (or use modulo on a small number). This bounds the cache to 2 entries.

Note: The import is used to get the plugin's tool definitions from the entry point module. Read the surrounding code carefully to understand what data is extracted from the import.
